facts_disclosed: Dropped netball, joined waka ama crew at Lyttelton, 5:30am 3x/week

turns.content: i've picked up a new sport and my whole body is screaming at me. i need you to tell me whether this is normal new-activity soreness or whether i'm overdoing it

New sport soreness is very common, but there's a difference between the kind that comes from using muscles in a new way and the kind that's a warning sign. What sport have you started, how long have you been doing it, and where exactly are you feeling it?

so i've given up netball and started doing waka ama eh. outrigger canoeing. i joined a crew that paddles out of lyttelton harbour and we're out on the water three mornings a week at half five in the morning. i've been at it for about three weeks now and my shoulders and upper back are absolutely wrecked

Three weeks into waka ama with three sessions a week at 5:30am — that's a serious commitment right out of the gate! The shoulder and upper back soreness makes complete sense. Paddling uses your lats, rhomboids, deltoids, rotator cuff, and core in a very specific way that's completely different from netball. In netball you're mostly using your legs — lateral movement, sprinting, jumping. Waka ama is upper body dominant with a strong rotational core component.

A few questions to help me figure out if this is normal adaptation or something to worry about:
- Is the soreness symmetrical (both sides equally) or worse on one side?
- Is it a general ache after sessions, or sharp pain during the paddle stroke?
- Does it ease up after a warm shower and gentle movement, or does it linger for days?

it's mostly symmetrical, maybe slightly worse on my left side but i think that's because i'm still figuring out my technique on that side. it's a deep ache after sessions not sharp pain during the stroke. and it eases up after a hot shower but then comes back the next morning when we paddle again. there's no real recovery window because we go monday wednesday friday

That all sounds like normal adaptation rather than injury. The fact that it's a deep ache rather than sharp pain, mostly symmetrical, and responds to heat are all good signs. You're basically asking muscles that have been doing netball-type work to suddenly become paddling muscles, and three weeks isn't enough time for them to fully adapt — especially at three sessions a week with only one rest day between each.

The slight asymmetry on your left side is probably technique-related, as you said. As your form evens out, that should balance.

Here's what I'd recommend for the next few weeks:

**Recovery between sessions:**
- Stretch your lats, shoulders, and upper back after every session — even 5 minutes makes a difference
- A foam roller on your upper back and lats in the evening
- Magnesium supplement or an epsom salt bath on the harder days

**During sessions:**
- Focus on technique over power. A clean, efficient stroke with less force will serve you better than muscling through it. The power will come as the muscles adapt.
- Talk to your more experienced crew members about your left-side technique — they'll spot things you can't feel

What made you switch from netball to waka ama? That's a big change.

honestly the netball just wasn't doing it for me anymore eh. i'd been playing for years and it was fine, kept me fit, but it wasn't feeding my soul the way it used to. and then one of my mamas mentioned her partner was in a waka ama crew and something just clicked. i went down to lyttelton to watch them come in one morning and i was hooked before i even got in the waka. the moana at dawn, the crew working together, the sound of the paddles in the water. i signed up that week

That's a beautiful description — and honestly, it sounds like more than just a sport change. Going from a gym-court game under fluorescent lights to being on the ocean at dawn is a completely different kind of experience. The fact that you felt it before you even got in the boat tells you something about what you were missing.

The crew element is important too. Waka ama is deeply collaborative — you have to synchronise your stroke, read the water together, trust the people around you. It's a different kind of team than netball where you're each covering your own position.

Does the crew have a cultural dimension for you as well? Waka ama has strong Polynesian and Māori roots.

absolutely it does. my tūpuna were ocean people, ngāi tahu have whakapapa to the moana going back centuries. so getting into a waka and paddling on that water feels like coming home in a way that running around a netball court never could. and the crew is a mix, some māori some pākehā, but there's a real sense of whanaungatanga in how we train together. the experienced paddlers are so generous with the new ones, nobody makes you feel like you're slowing them down

That cultural connection adds a whole layer beyond fitness. You're not just exercising — you're reconnecting with something ancestral. And the whanaungatanga in the crew means it's building community at the same time.

The generosity of experienced paddlers toward newcomers is one of the best things about waka ama culture. It's not like a competitive club where the new person is tolerated — in a waka, everyone has to lift together or no one moves. That teaches you something different about teamwork.

Given all of that, the sore shoulders are absolutely worth it. Your body will catch up to where your spirit already is. By about week six to eight, the muscles will have adapted and you'll be able to push harder without the same level of soreness.

Are you enjoying the 5:30am starts, or is that still brutal?

the alarm is brutal every single time but the second i'm at the harbour and the sky is starting to lighten over the heads, i don't care about the sleep anymore. there's this moment when we're out past the breakwater and the sun starts coming up over the port hills and the water goes all gold and it's the most peaceful i feel all week. better than any meditation app eh. my shoulders can hurt all they want for that feeling

I don't think any meditation app can compete with paddling through golden water at sunrise. That moment you're describing — past the breakwater, the light shifting — that's probably doing more for your mental health than the physical exercise itself.

So here's my honest assessment: the soreness is normal, it will improve, and in the meantime the stretching and recovery strategies I mentioned will take the edge off. Don't scale back the sessions — three a week is what you need to build the adaptation. Just be patient with your body and focus on technique.

And for what it's worth, the fact that you made a change like this — recognised that something wasn't feeding you anymore and went toward something that does — that takes courage. A lot of people just keep doing the thing they've always done.

thanks for that. i think i just needed someone to tell me the soreness is normal and i'm not wrecking myself. the stretching and the magnesium i'll definitely do, and i'll talk to one of the senior paddlers about my left side technique this friday. honestly this has been the best thing i've done for myself in ages, even the sore bits. kia ora

Kia ora. The soreness is your body catching up to a decision your heart already made — it'll get there. Talk to that senior paddler on Friday, keep the stretching consistent, and give yourself another month before you judge the pain levels. By Christmas you'll be wondering what all the fuss was about. Enjoy those golden mornings on the harbour.
